items.reality: L1/L2 help when variance or weight instability drives poor generalization; with abundant clean data and a well-specified simple model, λ ≈ 0 may be optimal and regularization adds unnecessary bias.
items.example: On 50,000 rows and 8 weakly correlated features, CV may select λ near zero and regularized models match unregularized test MSE.

items.myth: L1 and L2 are interchangeable—just pick one.
items.reality: They induce different weight geometries: L2 shrinks all weights smoothly; L1 promotes exact zeros. The choice depends on whether you need sparsity or stable retention of correlated features.
items.example: 200 collinear polynomial terms: L2 keeps all with moderated weights; L1 may zero most and keep an arbitrary subset.

items.myth: A larger λ is always safer against overfitting.
items.reality: Excessive λ over-shrinks weights, raising bias and underfitting—both training and validation error can climb together.
items.example: At λ=1e6 every linear coefficient nears zero; predictions collapse toward the mean with high validation MSE.

items.myth: Regularization fixes bad features or label noise.
items.reality: Penalties limit model complexity but cannot invent signal from irrelevant inputs or correct mislabeled data; feature engineering and data quality still matter.
items.example: Adding L2 to a model with leaked target columns still overfits—the leak dominates regardless of penalty strength.
